summary: Riccomini reflects on the evolution of data pipelines, noting that simpler batch ETL approaches are making a comeback for smaller organizations. He references his 2019 QCon talk outlining stages of pipeline maturity, emphasizing that many companies don't need to progress beyond basic batch processing. The post highlights how pipeline ownership has shifted from dedicated data engineering teams to ML, AI, and product engineers who prefer Python-centric tools. He introduces dlt (data load tool) as a promising solution that fits modern workflows while incorporating SDLC best practices. While acknowledging challenges like duplication and unclear ownership, Riccomini sees this democratization of data pipelines as a positive trend driven by data consumers wanting more control.
key_points: Most organizations progress through predictable stages of data pipeline maturity, but many don't need to advance beyond basic batch processing
WePay successfully ran a 15-minute interval batch pipeline on Airflow for years, proving sophisticated tooling isn't always necessary
Pipeline ownership has shifted from dedicated data engineering teams to ML, AI, analytics, and product engineers
Tools like dlt aim to bring the Python library revolution that transformed ML/AI to the data engineering space
Decentralized pipeline ownership creates challenges around duplication, inefficiency, and unclear ownership as employees come and go
money_quotes: For many smaller organizations, these tools are overkill.
Developers are thinking: if I can get by with a small query engine (DuckDB), maybe I can get by with a small ETL tool.
These days, we're asking ML, AI, analytics, and product engineers to build data pipelines.
It ran at 15m intervals and it was remarkably effective.
Data consumers want control of their data pipelines.
If everyone is managing their own data pipelines, there will be duplication.
The pipelines could become a tangled mess with no clear ownership as employees come and go.
key_insight: The democratization of data pipelines through lightweight, Python-native tools like dlt reflects a broader shift where data consumers increasingly want direct control over their data flows, even if it means accepting some organizational messiness.
